ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tandoori chicken; closest_parent 'chicken food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated tandoori chicken entry; A00LC (Chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent code.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of tandoori chicken in Singapore is a half bird weighing approximately 400 g of edible meat, served with mint chutney, sliced onions, and lemon wedges.

nutrient_source.title: Tandoori Chicken — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition), Table: Poultry and Egg Products
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN 2017 reports tandoori chicken (cooked, bone-in, skin-on) per 100 g edible portion. Values used directly. Serving-level GL calculated using 400 g serving size and GI of 30.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tandoori chicken. GI of 30 is estimated from the closest analogous food: grilled/roasted chicken with spice marinade, which is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food. Protein-dominant foods with minimal carbohydrate content consistently yield GI values in the 20–35 range per Atkinson et al. 2021 and Foster-Powell et al. 2002 (Am J Clin Nutr 76:5–56). GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 8.4) / 100 = 2.52, rounded to 2.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from marinade spices and salt (~420 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to a quarter chicken (~200 g) to keep sodium intake from this dish below 840 mg per meal, and request reduced-salt preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.1 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (~30)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tandoori chicken is a suitable protein-rich, low-GI choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; a standard half-chicken serving contributes approximately 8.4 g of carbohydrate and a glycemic load of only 2.5, posing minimal postprandial glucose impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.4 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (~85 mg per 100 g) from skin-on chicken
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer skinless tandoori chicken to reduce saturated fat and cholesterol intake, and pair the dish with high-fibre accompaniments such as salad or whole-grain bread to improve the overall dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori Chicken is a classic North Indian dish in which whole or portioned spring chicken is marinated in a mixture of plain yoghurt, freshly ground spices (including red chilli, cumin, coriander, garam masala, and turmeric), ginger, garlic, lemon juice, and salt, then cooked at very high temperature in a traditional clay oven called a tandoor. The intense dry heat of the tandoor — reaching 400–480 °C — chars the exterior slightly while sealing in moisture, producing a distinctive smoky aroma, a vivid red-orange colour from the chilli and turmeric, and a tender, juicy interior. The dish is typically served with mint-coriander chutney, thinly sliced raw onion rings, lemon wedges, and sometimes naan or roti. Nutritionally, tandoori chicken is a high-protein, moderate-fat, very low-carbohydrate food. Per 100 g edible portion it provides approximately 185 kcal, 24.5 g protein, 8.8 g fat (of which 2.4 g is saturated), and only 2.1 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is low (estimated ~30), making it an excellent choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or seeking blood-glucose control. The sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for those with hypertension. In Singapore, tandoori chicken is widely available at North Indian restaurants and is commonly ordered as a half or full bird, priced between SGD 19.90 and SGD 26.25. It is naturally gluten-free and halal when prepared with compliant ingredients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tandoori chicken?
Is tandoori chicken good for diabetics?
Is tandoori chicken high in protein?
What spices are in tandoori chicken?
Can I eat tandoori chicken if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN 2017 Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, which is the primary approved regional source for Indian dishes and contains a specific tandoori chicken entry. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021, clearly documented as such. Ontology anchors are defensible. Multilingual names verified for all five required languages (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
notes: Serving size reflects a half-chicken portion (~400 g edible meat) as commonly sold at Singapore restaurants (SGD 19.90–26.25). Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN 2017 source for this food item. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN 2017 source for this food item. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN 2017 source for this food item. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g (trace level from animal fat); this is a naturally occurring trans fat from ruminant-adjacent poultry fat, not industrial hydrogenation. The GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for tandoori chicken was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date. GL per serving = (30 × 8.4 g carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 = 2.52, rounded to 2.5. SNOMED CT code 226529007 'Tandoori chicken (substance)' is used as an exact match. FoodOn and FoodEx2 use closest_parent codes as no exact tandoori chicken term exists in those ontologies. Halal status assumes restaurant preparation with halal-certified chicken and no alcohol in marinade, as is standard at the listed Singapore restaurants. The food_name is normalised to 'Tandoori Chicken' (dropping the Half/Full qualifier) as the canonical English name; the Half/Full variants are captured in aliases.
